title: Real-World Effectiveness and Safety of Escalating Once-Weekly Semaglutide from 0.5 to 1.0 mg in Type 2 Diabetes
text:
[DOCUMENT METADATA]
Title: Real-World Effectiveness and Safety of Escalating Once-Weekly Semaglutide from 0.5 to 1.0 mg in Type 2 Diabetes
Year: 2026
Type: observational_study
Drugs: semaglutide
Keywords: not specified
Domain: Type 2 Diabetes Drug Interactions
[END METADATA]

[SECTION: INTRODUCTION]
The global prevalence of type 2 diabetes (T2DM), closely related to the parallel epidemic of obesity, is a substantial public health challenge [ 1 ]. The management of T2DM has evolved beyond glycemic control to a comprehensive strategy aimed at reducing the burden of cardiovascular, renal, and other major complications that contribute substantially to morbidity and mortality in this population [ 2 ,  3 ]. Therefore, current guidelines recommend selecting glucose-lowering agents with proven cardiovascular and renal benefits [ 3 ]. In this therapeutic landscape, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) have become a cornerstone of pharmacologic management, distinguished by their ability to improve glycemic control, promote weight loss, and reduce major adverse cardiovascular events in randomized outcome trials [ 4 – 9 ]. Among these agents, once-weekly (OW) injectable semaglutide has emerged as a particularly potent option, with robust evidence from the Semaglutide Unabated Sustainability in Treatment of Type 2 Diabetes (SUSTAIN) phase 3 program supporting its efficacy across diverse clinical settings [ 5 ,  10 – 18 ]. Although these trials have established the broad benefits of semaglutide, its optimal clinical application is based on understanding the dose–response relationship across commonly used maintenance doses. To date, several SUSTAIN phase 3 trials that included both 0.5-mg and 1.0-mg arms (e.g., SUSTAIN 1, 2, 4, 5, and 7) consistently showed higher reductions in glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) levels and body weight with the 1.0-mg dose than with the 0.5-mg dose, supporting a clinically relevant incremental benefit of escalating the dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg. However, these randomized trials did not directly address the incremental effectiveness and tolerability of increasing the semaglutide dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg in heterogeneous patient populations typically encountered in routine clinical practice.
Randomized clinical trials (RCTs) provide internally valid estimates of efficacy and safety in carefully selected populations under controlled conditions; however, their generalizability to routine clinical practice can be limited by restrictive inclusion criteria and standardized care protocols. Observational real-world evidence (RWE) complements the findings of RCTs by describing effectiveness, tolerability, and treatment patterns in heterogeneous patient populations managed in everyday settings. Furthermore, several RWE studies have confirmed that OW semaglutide provides clinically meaningful improvements in glycemia and weight in routine practice [ 19 – 23 ]. However, real-world prescription patterns indicate that escalation to the 1.0-mg dose is not consistently performed. For example, the multinational SURE program demonstrated substantial variation in the use of the 1.0-mg dose, with the proportion of patients maintained on this dose ranging from over 70% in Denmark and Spain to approximately 38% in Italy [ 20 ,  24 ,  25 ]. Furthermore, observational reports from Japan suggest an even lower rate of escalation, with many patients remaining on the 0.25- or 0.5-mg dose [ 21 ]. These observations highlight a significant gap between the theoretical benefits of full-dose therapy and the realities in clinical practice, in which many patients are maintained on lower OW semaglutide doses (0.25–0.5 mg) without escalation to 1.0 mg. Clinicians frequently have to decide whether to escalate the dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg to achieve additional benefit while maintaining tolerability; however, uncertainty regarding the magnitude of incremental effect and risk of adverse events (AEs) may contribute to reluctance to escalate the dose. Although our previous real-world study suggested that escalating the oral semaglutide dose from 7 to 14 mg may provide additional metabolic benefits with acceptable tolerability [ 26 ], it remains unclear whether a similar dose-escalation strategy with OW injectable semaglutide would yield consistent incremental improvements.
To the best of our knowledge, no real-world study has specifically quantified the incremental effectiveness and tolerability of escalating the OW semaglutide dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg in patients treated in routine clinical practice. To address this evidence gap and provide clinically relevant data to guide this common therapeutic decision, we aimed to evaluate the real-world effectiveness and safety of escalating the OW semaglutide dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg in a single-center retrospective cohort. We hypothesized that, in routine practice, escalation from 0.5 to 1.0 mg would be associated with additional improvements in HbA1c level and body weight, without introducing new safety concerns.

[SECTION: METHODS]
This was a single-center, retrospective cohort study involving adults with type 2 diabetes who received an escalated once-weekly semaglutide dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg in routine clinical care. The primary outcome was within-patient change in glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level 24 weeks after the initial prescription of 1-mg semaglutide. Secondary outcomes included changes in metabolic parameters and the incidence of newly documented adverse events after escalation to 1.0 mg. Efficacy was analyzed in a prespecified on-treatment set, and safety was assessed in an all-exposed set.

[SECTION: RESULTS]
In the efficacy set ( n  = 126), the HbA1c level decreased by 0.40% ± 0.70% and body weight by 2.2 ± 2.7 kg at week 24 (both  p  < 0.01). Additionally, reductions were observed in alanine aminotransferase and γ-glutamyl transpeptidase levels, total number of concomitant glucose-lowering agents, and total daily insulin dose. In the safety set ( n  = 128), gastrointestinal adverse events were the most frequent (20.3%), particularly nausea (14.1%). Dose reduction was required in 12.5% of patients, mostly owing to gastrointestinal symptoms, and discontinuation for any reason occurred in 5.5%; only one patient (0.8%) discontinued treatment because of an adverse event. No serious drug-related adverse events were recorded.

[SECTION: CONCLUSIONS]
In this real-world cohort, escalating the dose of OW semaglutide from 0.5 to 1.0 mg was associated with additional reductions in the HbA1c level and body weight without serious AEs. Although the single-arm design without a comparator group maintained on the 0.5-mg dose precludes causal inference regarding incremental benefit, these findings provide RWE that may support clinical decision-making regarding dose intensification in patients with suboptimal control on 0.5-mg semaglutide.

Supplementary Information:
Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material. Supplementary file1 (PDF 261 KB)

Key Summary Points:
Why carry out this study? 
 There is a lack of clear, dose-specific evidence on the incremental effectiveness and tolerability of escalating once-weekly semaglutide dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg, limiting routine dose-escalation decision-making by clinicians. This study evaluated whether escalation to 1.0 mg in routine clinical practice was associated with additional glycemic and weight benefits and assessed real-world safety after dose escalation. 
 What was learned from the study? 
 Escalation to 1.0 mg was associated with additional improvements at 24 weeks (HbA1c level: − 0.40% ± 0.70%; body weight: − 2.2 ± 2.7 kg; both  p  < 0.01). Gastrointestinal adverse events were the most common safety findings (20.3%); dose reduction occurred in 12.5% of patients, treatment discontinuation for any reason in 5.5%, and treatment discontinuation because of an adverse event in 0.8%. Although causal inference is limited by the single-arm retrospective design, these findings may help inform dose-intensification decisions in routine practice.

Study Design and Participants:
This was a retrospective, single-center, observational cohort study conducted using medical records from the Division of Diabetes, Metabolism and Endocrinology, Toho University Omori Medical Center, a tertiary-care university hospital in Tokyo, Japan. The study period spanned from June 2020, the month of semaglutide’s marketing launch in Japan, to the data cutoff in March 2025. We screened all adult patients (aged ≥ 18 years) with T2DM who received a prescription for OW injectable semaglutide and whose dose was escalated from 0.5 to 1.0 mg during the study interval.
Patients were excluded if they had no evaluable baseline assessment data during stable treatment with the 0.5-mg dose prior to escalation; had insufficient post-escalation follow-up by the data cutoff to contribute 24-week assessments (defined as having initiated the 1.0-mg dose less than 18 weeks prior to March 10, 2025); had received OW semaglutide in a manner inconsistent with the approved product label (e.g., non-standard dosing schedule or indication); had been hospitalized for ≥ 1 week during the data collection period in which inpatient glycemic management was provided; had started semaglutide at another institution, thus preventing reliable confirmation of a complete dosing history; were lost to follow-up before any post-escalation assessment could be obtained within the first scheduled 12-week assessment window (operationally, before 6 weeks after escalation), such that no follow-up data were available after dose escalation; had excessive alcohol consumption (defined as a mean daily intake of pure alcohol > 60 g for men or > 30 g for women); had dementia; became pregnant during the data collection period; received maintenance dialysis at baseline; demonstrated clinically significant non-adherence to medication, as judged by the treating physician, based on documentation in the medical record indicating that semaglutide use was too infrequent or inconsistent for meaningful evaluation; or had type 1 diabetes mellitus or another form of diabetes judged by the attending physician to be unsuitable for inclusion in this T2DM cohort.
Patients whose dose was reduced before any evaluable efficacy assessment could be obtained were included in the safety analysis but excluded from the efficacy analysis.

Data Sources and Variables:
Clinical and laboratory data were extracted from the electronic medical records and entered into a de-identified study database. Baseline values were defined as the most recent measurement from the 12-week period to the index date (the first prescription of 1.0-mg semaglutide). Follow-up measurements were performed at 12 (± 6) and 24 (± 6) weeks after escalation; the measurement closest to the target date was used for analysis (Fig.  1 ). Fig. 1 Study design. The index date indicates the initial prescription date for 1 mg of once-weekly injectable semaglutide. The baseline period was defined as 12 weeks before the index date. For each outcome, 12 and 24 weeks after the index date and baseline were compared
We extracted a comprehensive set of variables for each patient. First, we collected demographic and anthropometric data, including age, sex, weight, height, and body mass index (BMI). Additionally, we collated an extensive panel of clinical and laboratory parameters, such as diabetes duration, blood pressure, glycemic marker (HbA1c, plasma glucose) levels, liver enzyme [alanine aminotransferase (ALT), γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (γ-GTP)] levels, renal function [serum creatinine level, estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR)], lipid profile, and uric acid level. In addition, we recorded medication data, including all glucose-lowering agents and total daily insulin dose. Safety and tolerability outcomes, including AEs, dose reductions, and discontinuations, were captured from physician progress notes and prescription records. No standardized AE questionnaire or formal adjudication process was used. AEs were defined as newly documented events after escalation to 1.0 mg; symptoms during the preceding 0.5-mg treatment period were not systematically collected in this study and were, therefore, not available for formal comparison. The assessment of AE severity, causality, and time-to-onset was limited by the nature of routine chart documentation.

Outcomes:
The primary outcome was the within-patient change in the HbA1c level from baseline to 24 weeks after escalation to 1.0-mg semaglutide. Secondary outcomes included a range of clinical and metabolic parameters assessed over the 24-week period. These encompassed changes in anthropometric measures (body weight, BMI) and blood pressure, as well as changes in laboratory markers for liver and renal function, lipid profile, and uric acid level.
Further efficacy outcomes included the proportion of patients attaining an HbA1c level < 7.0% and the percentages achieving weight loss ≥ 3% and ≥ 5% from baseline. These thresholds were selected because obesity-related health parameters improved in Japanese overweight and obese individuals achieving weight loss of 3% to < 5% and ≥ 5%, with 3% identified as the minimum clinically meaningful threshold [ 27 ]. Additionally, we evaluated changes in therapeutic regimens, such as the number of concomitant glucose-lowering agents and total daily insulin dose. The incidence of all AEs was recorded to assess safety and tolerability.

Statistical Analysis:
Continuous variables were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Categorical variables were summarized as counts and percentages. Paired  t  tests and McNemar’s test were used to analyze the changes in continuous and categorical outcomes, respectively, between the baseline and follow-up. For exploratory analysis of factors associated with the treatment response, we used a series of simple linear regressions to examine the association between various baseline covariates and the change in HbA1c level or body weight at 24 weeks. The baseline covariates assessed included demographic and clinical characteristics (age, sex, diabetes duration, and the duration from 0.5 to 1.0 mg initiation); laboratory values [HbA1c, creatinine, eGFR, ALT, and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) levels]; anthropometric measures (BMI); and concomitant medication data [number of glucose-lowering agents, total daily insulin dose, and use of sodium/glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT-2) inhibitors, insulin, or biguanides].
The study was not powered for complex multivariable modeling, and the sample size was modest; therefore, multivariable regression was not performed. In post hoc exploratory subgroup analyses, patients were stratified according to the time from initiation of OW 0.5-mg semaglutide to the first prescription of 1.0-mg semaglutide. To explore the potential impact of titration timing on clinical outcomes, “early” and “late” escalation groups were defined using the cohort median as the cutoff, as there is presently no established clinical consensus defining “rapid” versus “delayed” escalation. These subgroup comparisons were intended to be descriptive and hypothesis-generating; no formal tests of interaction were performed and no multivariable adjustment was applied for baseline imbalances. A two-sided  p  value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Given the observational and exploratory nature of this study, no formal adjustment for multiplicity was applied to the secondary or exploratory outcomes; therefore,  p  values for these outcomes should be interpreted cautiously and considered hypothesis-generating.
The primary estimand of this study was the on-treatment effect of escalation to 1.0-mg semaglutide, defined as the change in outcomes while patients continued receiving the 1.0-mg dose. Accordingly, observations after dose reduction or treatment discontinuation were excluded from efficacy analyses but included in safety summaries. We did not estimate a treatment-policy (all-exposed/intention-to-treat-like) effect, as this represents a different estimand and would require additional prespecified analytic assumptions to appropriately handle treatment modification and its potentially non-random occurrence in routine care. Such analyses were beyond the scope of the present study. The study analyses were conducted using SAS version 9.4 (Cary, NC).

Ethics Statement:
All procedures in this study were performed in accordance with the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki, as revised in 2024. The study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Faculty of Medicine, Toho University (No. T2024-2415). This study used retrospective non-identifiable data obtained by the treating physicians; therefore, based on the decision of the local Ethics Committee of Toho University Omori Medical Center, informed consent was not required. Patients were allowed to object to the use of their data for retrospective scientific research; however, none objected.

Cohort Derivation and Baseline Characteristics:
Figure  2  shows a flow diagram of participant screening, exclusions, and final cohort numbers. During the study period, 246 patients were prescribed 1.0-mg semaglutide at our center. After applying the predefined exclusion criteria, 128 patients were included in the safety cohort. The efficacy cohort for primary analyses included 126 patients; two individuals were excluded from the efficacy cohort because their dose was reduced to 0.5 mg immediately after escalation. Fig. 2 Flowchart of patient enrollment.  MODY  maturity-onset diabetes of the young
The baseline demographic and clinical characteristics of the 126 patients are presented in Table  1 . The mean age ± SD was 58.2 ± 11.0 years; 88 patients (69.8%) were male and 38 (30.2%) were female. Participants had a longstanding history of diabetes (14.5 ± 9.6 years) and a mean baseline BMI of 31.1 ± 5.3 kg/m 2 . Glycemic control at baseline was indicated by a mean HbA1c level of 7.7 ± 1.2%. Regarding background therapy, patients were receiving a mean of 1.9 ± 1.0 glucose-lowering agents in addition to OW semaglutide. The most common concomitant therapies were insulin (66%), SGLT2 inhibitors (60%), and biguanides (48%). Few patients were also receiving a dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitor at baseline; chart review suggested that these combinations reflected inadvertent continuation during co-management across departments rather than an intentional long-term combination strategy. Primary Outcome:
As shown in Fig.  3 , escalating the semaglutide dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg was associated with a reduction in the mean HbA1c level, from 7.7% ± 1.2% at baseline to 7.2% ± 0.9% at 24 weeks (mean change, − 0.4% ± 0.7%;  p  < 0.01). The reduction in the HbA1c level was observed early, with most of the improvement evident by the 12-week mark; this effect was sustained for 24 weeks. Fig. 3 Glycated hemoglobin level trajectory over 24 weeks. Values are presented as mean ± standard deviation. The numbers of patients were 126, 114, and 114 at 0, 12, and 24 weeks, respectively. * p  values indicate differences from baseline

Secondary Metabolic Outcomes:
We observed improvements in secondary glycemic outcomes. The proportion of patients who achieved an HbA1c level < 7.0% increased from 27.8% at baseline to 47.4% at 24 weeks ( p  < 0.01) (Table  2 ). In addition, body weight decreased progressively. The mean body weight decreased from 86.2 ± 17.9 kg at baseline to 84.8 ± 17.6 kg at 24 weeks ( p  < 0.01), with a corresponding reduction in BMI. At the 24-week follow-up, 41.5% of patients had lost ≥ 3% of their baseline body weight and 22.6% had lost ≥ 5%. Analysis of other laboratory and clinical parameters revealed several changes. A reduction was observed in triglyceride levels ( p  < 0.01), whereas no material changes were observed in other lipid profiles. Markers of hepatic function improved, with reductions in ALT and γ-GTP levels. Renal function remained stable, with no material changes in serum creatinine or eGFR levels. Although there was no observable change in the UACR, a detailed evaluation was difficult because of the small sample size and high variability in the data. Blood pressure and uric acid levels remained largely unchanged.
Regarding concomitant medications, the mean number of glucose-lowering agents decreased from 1.9 ± 1.0 at baseline to 1.8 ± 1.0 at 24 weeks ( p  < 0.05). The total daily insulin dose among insulin-treated patients significantly decreased over the 24-week period (from 31.3 ± 19.8 units to 30.0 ± 19.3 units;  p  < 0.01).

Safety and Tolerability:
The safety and tolerability profile is summarized in Table  3 . Overall, at least one AE was reported in 44.5% of patients in the safety cohort. The most frequent AEs were gastrointestinal disorders, occurring in 20.3% of patients; nausea was the single most common complaint (14.1%), followed by diarrhea (4.7%). Hypoglycemia was reported in 5.5% of patients ( n  = 7); six cases occurred in patients receiving concomitant insulin and one occurred in a patient receiving a concomitant glinide. Among insulin users ( n  = 83), hypoglycemia occurred in 7.2%. Treatment modifications were required in a subset of patients. The dose was reduced from 1.0 to 0.5 mg in 12.5% of patients, primarily because of gastrointestinal disorders. Although permanent discontinuation of therapy was recorded in 5.5% of patients, the reasons varied and included elective switches to other incretin-based agents, perceived inadequate efficacy, and patient request. Notably, only one discontinuation was directly attributable to a drug side effect, which was injection site pain. No serious drug-related AEs were observed during the study period. These AEs represent newly documented events after escalation to 1.0 mg. Comparable AE data during the preceding 0.5-mg treatment period were not systematically collected in this study. Subgroup Analyses:
In the exploratory subgroup analysis assessing the impact of titration timing, patients were stratified by the median interval to dose escalation (70 days) into an early (< 70 days) and a late group (≥ 70 days), with outcomes summarized in Supplementary Material (Tables S1 and S2). Regarding the analysis populations, the early escalation group included 62 patients in the efficacy set and 63 in the safety set; the late escalation group included 64 and 65 patients in the efficacy and safety sets, respectively. Baseline glycemic control was similar (mean HbA1c level: 7.7% vs. 7.6%), whereas the early group had higher body weight and BMI (90.6 kg and 32.1 kg/m 2  vs. 81.3 kg and 30.1 kg/m 2 ).
Despite these baseline imbalances, both groups achieved within-group reductions in HbA1c levels and body weight at 24 weeks. However, a reduction in total daily insulin dose was only observed in the early titration group (− 3.8 units;  p  < 0.01). Regarding tolerability, dose reductions because of gastrointestinal AEs occurred in 3.2% and 10.8% of patients in the early and late groups, respectively. Given the post hoc nature of this stratification, baseline imbalances between the groups, and lack of multivariable adjustment, these subgroup findings should be interpreted as descriptive and hypothesis-generating only.

Exploratory Analysis of Factors Associated with Response:
In the exploratory analysis, several baseline characteristics were associated with the 24-week response to dose escalation (Table S3). Regarding the change in the HbA1c level, a greater reduction was associated with two baseline factors: a higher HbA1c level (regression coefficient, − 0.333;  p  < 0.001) and older age (− 0.013;  p  < 0.05). Conversely, a higher baseline ALT level was associated with a smaller reduction in the HbA1c level (0.006;  p  < 0.05). Two factors associated with the change in body weight were identified. A higher baseline ALT level was associated with lesser weight loss (0.027;  p  < 0.01). In contrast, a higher baseline HDL-C level was associated with greater weight loss (− 0.043;  p  < 0.05). No other baseline variables, including BMI and diabetes duration, showed a clear association with the change in either outcome; these univariable findings should be interpreted as exploratory correlates rather than as definitive predictors of treatment response.

[SECTION: DISCUSSION]
In this single-center, retrospective, real-world cohort study, escalating the OW semaglutide dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg was associated with additional reductions in the HbA1c level and body weight at 24 weeks. Furthermore, the dose escalation was associated with decreases in ALT and γ-GTP levels and with reductions in the number of concomitant glucose-lowering agents and total daily insulin dose. The overall AE profile—predominantly gastrointestinal symptoms—was comparable to that reported in previous randomized and observational studies; only one discontinuation was directly attributable to AEs. These observations are broadly consistent with those of our prior real-world study of oral semaglutide, where escalation from 7 to 14 mg was associated with incremental improvements in glycemic control and body weight, with no serious AEs leading to drug discontinuation [ 26 ]. Although direct comparisons between formulations are not feasible, these data support the hypothesis that semaglutide dose intensification may confer additional metabolic benefits across delivery routes in Japanese patients with T2DM.
Our findings suggest that, in clinical practice, escalating the semaglutide dose from 0.5 to 1.0 mg is associated with further improvements in glycemic control and weight loss. This pattern is consistent with dose–response relationships observed in RCTs of semaglutide, which reported greater glycemic and weight effects at higher doses [ 10 ,  11 ,  13 – 15 ]. Notably, because 0.5-mg semaglutide is already recognized as a highly efficacious dose, demonstrating additional benefit in patients who were stably treated at this level underscores the potential clinical value of escalation. Unlike RCT populations that are often treatment naive or tightly selected, our cohort reflects a heterogeneous, treatment-experienced real-world population. Accordingly, the absolute magnitudes of change may appear smaller than those reported in trials using treatment-initiation baselines. Nevertheless, the observed within-patient improvements after escalation remain clinically informative and support the clinical relevance of dose escalation to 1.0 mg when treatment goals are not achieved on 0.5 mg.
We observed reductions in ALT and γ-GTP levels after dose escalation. Previous pooled trial analyses and mechanistic studies have reported similar reductions in aminotransferases and inflammatory markers with semaglutide and other GLP-1 RAs; additionally, several randomized studies have demonstrated improvements in hepatic steatosis and metabolic markers in populations with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and non-alcoholic steatohepatitis [ 28 – 30 ]. These data suggest that semaglutide may improve hepatic metabolic stress partly through weight loss and systemic metabolic improvements, and direct hepatic effects have been proposed. However, we found that patients with higher baseline ALT levels tended to show smaller glycemic and weight responses after escalation. One plausible explanation is that advanced hepatic steatosis or fibrosis—conditions associated with more severe insulin resistance—may attenuate the metabolic responsiveness to incretin-based therapies. Alternatively, elevated baseline ALT levels may serve as a surrogate marker of increased hepatic steatosis and insulin resistance, conditions associated with greater systemic insulin resistance and reduced metabolic responsiveness to incretin-based therapies. This interpretation is supported by observational and interventional studies linking higher ALT levels to Homeostatic Model Assessment for Insulin Resistance, directly measured insulin resistance, and NAFLD [ 31 – 35 ]. These hypotheses require dedicated evaluation using objective liver assessments and stratified analyses.
No significant change in eGFR levels or albuminuria was observed during the 24-week follow-up. This lack of change in renal parameters was unsurprising because of the short follow-up duration; besides, kidney protection with GLP-1 RAs has largely been demonstrated in large RCTs with longer follow-up durations. Notably, a dedicated renal outcome trial showed that semaglutide reduced the risk of major kidney outcomes in patients with T2DM and established chronic kidney disease [ 36 ]. In contrast, baseline kidney function in our cohort was relatively preserved; therefore, our population differed from the more advanced CKD population in the FLOW trial, and the likelihood of detecting short-term renal benefits was reduced. The evaluation of changes in albuminuria was limited by the small number of patients with available UACR measurements and few patients underwent longitudinal, repeated assessments; therefore, changes in the UACR over time were difficult to assess. Taken together, the negative renal findings should be interpreted considering the insufficient follow-up duration, limited sample size, restricted availability of serial albuminuria data, and a baseline renal profile milder than that in the FLOW trial.
Gastrointestinal AEs were the most frequently observed AEs, consistent with previous findings [ 5 ,  10 – 18 ]. Importantly, no serious AEs were observed, and only one patient (0.8% of the safety cohort) discontinued treatment owing to mild injection site pain. These findings suggest that treatment discontinuation directly attributable to AEs was infrequent in this cohort; however, given the retrospective, chart-based ascertainment of AEs and the lack of systematically collected pre-escalation AE data, safety results should be interpreted as descriptive. The apparently higher frequency of dose reductions in the subgroup with delayed escalation may reflect selection bias—clinicians may have advanced escalation more cautiously in patients perceived to be at higher risk for gastrointestinal intolerance—as well as ascertainment bias or unmeasured confounding, rather than a direct causal effect of delayed escalation per se. Therefore, causal inference should be avoided. Because this early-versus-late escalation comparison was exploratory and post hoc, these findings should be interpreted as descriptive and hypothesis-generating. Accordingly, individualized titration and patient education remain important in routine care.
The present findings may have practical implications for the management of T2DM. Although individual responses undoubtedly vary, these real-world data indicate that escalation of OW semaglutide from 0.5 to 1.0 mg is associated with additional reductions in HbA1c level and body weight and may help inform dose intensification decisions when treatment goals are not achieved on a lower dose. In the exploratory univariable analysis, higher baseline HbA1c was associated with a greater HbA1c reduction after dose escalation; however, this association should be interpreted cautiously, as it may reflect greater room for improvement and regression to the mean rather than a subgroup-specific benefit of escalation to 1.0 mg. Both subgroups showed within-group improvements; however, given the single-arm design and potential confounding, the apparent consistency across escalation timing should be interpreted as descriptive and hypothesis-generating.
The strengths of this study include its real-world setting reflecting routine clinical decision-making and the comprehensive capture of clinically relevant outcomes beyond glycemic metrics, such as medication adjustments and AEs. The inclusion of patients with diverse characteristics, including those on insulin therapy, further enhances the applicability of our findings to daily clinical practice.
However, several limitations inherent to the study design warrant mention. First, the retrospective, single-center nature introduces potential selection and information biases. However, we mitigated selection bias by consecutively including all eligible patients who underwent dose escalation from the time of semaglutide launch. Second, although residual confounding cannot be excluded, the risk that concomitant co-interventions inflated efficacy estimates appears limited. In fact, the mean number of concomitant glucose-lowering agents and total daily insulin doses decreased during follow-up; therefore, our efficacy estimates are likely conservative rather than overestimated. Third, the study only included patients whose dose was escalated to 1.0 mg and lacked a control group of patients who maintained the 0.5-mg dose. Accordingly, we could not directly compare outcomes between dose escalation and continued 0.5-mg therapy or determine comparative effectiveness. Therefore, the findings should not be interpreted as demonstrating a causal incremental benefit of escalation. Moreover, because of the single-arm observational design, alternative explanations, including regression to the mean and time-dependent confounding, cannot be excluded. Fourth, this study did not evaluate comparative effectiveness or cost-effectiveness relative to other treatment strategies, including switching to or initiating other glucose-lowering agents such as GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonists. These issues remain to be addressed in future comparative studies. Fifth, sex-specific analyses were not performed because of the limited sample size and the retrospective single-center design of this study. Therefore, potential sex-related differences in the effectiveness or tolerability of dose escalation could not be assessed, and the applicability of these findings across women and men should be interpreted with caution. Sixth, regarding statistical limitations, the modest sample size and exploratory nature of the analyses limited the power for extensive multivariable adjustment and detection of rare safety events. Multiple secondary outcomes were evaluated without adjustment for multiplicity; therefore, these findings should be interpreted as hypothesis-generating. However, the key metabolic outcomes—HbA1c level, body weight, BMI, and total daily insulin dose—consistently showed highly significant improvements (nominal  p  < 0.01); therefore, these findings reflect a robust therapeutic signal rather than incidental findings (type I error). Seventh, our primary efficacy analyses targeted an on-treatment estimand and, therefore, may overestimate effectiveness compared with a treatment-policy (all-exposed) approach. A treatment-policy analysis would address a different clinical question and require additional analytical assumptions to account for treatment modification and its potentially non-random occurrence in routine practice. Accordingly, these findings should be interpreted as reflecting outcomes among patients who tolerated and continued using 1.0-mg semaglutide. Finally, there were safety and generalizability limitations. Because AEs were retrospectively identified from routine documentation rather than systematic surveillance, milder events may have been under-recorded and detailed severity or causality could not always be determined. Additionally, the high prevalence of insulin use in our cohort suggests that extrapolation to general primary care populations should be made with caution.
Based on these observations, large-scale, prospective, multicenter registry studies are warranted to confirm generalizability, particularly in primary care settings, and to provide sufficient power for robust multivariable modeling that can validate the exploratory factors associated with response. Furthermore, pragmatic randomized trials directly comparing dose escalation versus maintenance of the 0.5-mg dose with longer-term follow-up are required to establish higher-level evidence on comparative effectiveness and tolerability, including rare AEs.
